title: Density & Floating
subtitle: Exploring why things float and sink

questions.question: What does density measure?
options: The mass per unit volume of a substance
The weight of an object in water
questions.correctAnswer: The mass per unit volume of a substance
questions.explanation: Density is defined as mass divided by volume (D = m/v). It tells us how much matter is packed into a given space.

questions.question: An object will float in water if its density is:
questions.explanation: Water has a density of 1.0 g/cm³. Any object with a lower density will float; any object with a higher density will sink.

questions.question: Which of the following objects would sink in water?
questions.explanation: Iron has a density of about 7.87 g/cm³, which is much greater than water's 1.0 g/cm³, so it sinks.

questions.question: Why does a large steel ship float even though steel is denser than water?
options: Steel becomes less dense in salt water
The ship's hollow hull lowers its overall density below water
The weight of the water pushes the ship up
questions.correctAnswer: The ship's hollow hull lowers its overall density below water
questions.explanation: The hollow hull means the ship displaces a large volume of water. Its total mass divided by total volume gives an overall density less than 1.0 g/cm³.

questions.question: If you compress a floating object to half its volume without changing its mass, what happens?
options: It still floats — mass didn't change
It sinks — its density has doubled
It floats higher — it's smaller now
questions.correctAnswer: It sinks — its density has doubled
questions.explanation: Density = mass / volume. Halving the volume while keeping mass the same doubles the density, which may push it above 1.0 g/cm³ and cause it to sink.

steps.label: Orientation
steps.guidingQuestion: Why do some objects float while others sink?

poll.subtext: Share your initial thought before we explore

steps.label: Hypothesis
steps.framingQuestion: What will happen if we increase the density of an object placed in water?
steps.inputSubtext: Write what you think will happen and why.

peers.name: Alex M.
peers.text: I think it will sink faster because the density pushes it down.

peers.name: Sam K.
peers.text: No change will happen — temperature might matter more.

peers.name: Jordan P.
peers.text: If density increases beyond the liquid, it sinks.

steps.label: Experiment
objectives: Observe how changing an object's mass affects whether it floats or sinks.
Compare the density of different materials to water (1.0 g/cm³).
Record what happens when density goes above and below 1.0 g/cm³.
steps.observationPrompt: What did you notice when density increased?

postSimQuiz.question: What happens to an object when its density is greater than water (1.0 g/cm³)?
Which variable most directly determines if an object floats?
A hollow steel ship floats because its overall density is:

steps.label: Discussion
steps.reflectionPrompt: Did your results match your hypothesis? Why or why not?

classDiscussion.name: Mfariso Ibokette
classDiscussion.text: I hope to learn how to secure my local area network.

classDiscussion.name: Mr Okon James

classDiscussion.text: I noticed the object sank immediately once the density slider passed 1.0.

classDiscussion.name: Charity Musare
classDiscussion.text: With experience across STEM education platforms, I design with clarity and measurable impact.

examples.title: Ships Floating on Water
examples.description: Despite being made of heavy steel, ships are designed with hollow hulls to reduce overall density below that of water.

examples.title: Hot Air Balloons
examples.description: Heating air inside the balloon reduces its density, making it lighter than the surrounding cooler air.

examples.title: Oil & Water Separation
examples.description: Oil floats on water because it has a lower density. This principle is used in environmental cleanup.

optionalTask.prompt: Find one real-world example near you where density plays a role. Describe it below or upload a photo!
